1:
On Numbers 34:1 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark God himself fixing the borders of the promised land.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the land of Canaan is here described — its south, west, north, and east borders — to be divided by lot among the nine tribes and a half. The Puritan Henry: God directs Moses to settle the bounds, not as a geographer to please the curious, but as a prince in his grant. The Reformed Gill: spoken when God ordered Israel to drive out the inhabitants and divide the land. Calvin: God undertakes the office of a prudent father, fixing the boundaries lest their right to possess be ever questioned.

The verse opens the grant. God speaks to fix the borders of Canaan. The Lord himself bounds his people’s inheritance.

So the witnesses see God himself take in hand the fixing of the borders of the land he had promised, like a lord settling a grant upon his heirs. The witnesses note the timing Gill observes: the word came when God was ordering Israel, once over Jordan, to drive out the inhabitants of Canaan and divide the land among themselves; the bounds of the inheritance are now set down. They mark Henry’s fine observation on the purpose: God directs Moses to settle the line of the land, not as a geographer drawing a map merely to please the curious, but as a prince in his grant, conveying a sure title; the description is a deed of possession, marking out exactly what God gave. They observe Calvin’s like figure: that God here undertakes the office of a prudent and careful father of a family, fixing the boundaries on every side, lest the people’s right to possess the land should ever afterward be called in question; the bounds, set by God, made the title secure. They note the comfort the witnesses see in this divine bounding: that the inheritance of God’s people was no uncertain or disputed thing, but a land measured and granted by the LORD himself, who had promised it to Abraham long before (Genesis 15:18). They observe that the God who appoints to all nations the bounds of their habitation (Acts 17:26) here marks out the portion of his own. They note that the grant rested on God’s word, not on Israel’s sword. The verse thus opens the grant: God fixes the borders — the land named in the next verse.
2:
On Numbers 34:2 — “Command the children of Israel… When ye come into the land of Canaan; this is the land that shall fall unto you for an inheritance… with the coasts thereof” — the witnesses mark the land of Canaan given as an inheritance.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: when ye come into the land of Canaan, this is the land that shall fall to you for an inheritance. Adam Clarke: the boundaries known only by survey or a good map. The Reformed Gill: God himself fixed the bounds, having determined the times and bounds of men’s habitations (Acts 17:26). Wesley: the coasts particularly described, to bound their wars, that they seek not enlargement like other empires.

The verse names the land. Canaan, with its coasts, is the inheritance that shall fall to them. The bounds of the promise are set.

So the witnesses see the land of Canaan named as the inheritance God appointed to fall to Israel. The witnesses note the renewed promise: “when ye come into the land of Canaan; this is the land that shall fall unto you for an inheritance” — the land is spoken of as certainly to be possessed, the inheritance that should “fall” to them (by lot, as the chapter shows) from the hand of God. They mark Gill’s observation that the fixing of the bounds was God’s own act, not man’s, for it is he who has determined the times before appointed and the bounds of men’s habitations (Acts 17:26); the borders of Israel’s land were set in heaven before they were possessed on earth. They observe the careful describing of the coasts, and the reason Wesley draws out: the limits were particularly marked to direct and bound Israel in their wars and conquests, that they might not, after the manner of other empires, seek the boundless enlargement of their dominion, but rest content with the land God had given; the bounds were a curb on ambition as well as a charter of possession. They note Clarke’s plain remark that the exact line can be known only by survey or a good map, the names being ancient and many now uncertain. They observe the typology the witnesses see: that as God appointed Israel a bounded inheritance, so he has prepared for his people a better and abiding inheritance, the true Canaan (Hebrews 11:16). They note that the land fell to them by gift, not conquest only. The verse thus names the land of Canaan as their inheritance — the south border traced in the next verses.
3:
On Numbers 34:3 — “Then your south quarter shall be from the wilderness of Zin… and your south border shall be the outmost coast of the salt sea eastward” — the witnesses mark the south border.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the salt sea, the Dead Sea, or lake Asphaltites. The Reformed Gill: the south border ran from east to west. Wesley: described from east to west by windings, by reason of the mountains and rivers; the salt sea, so called from the salt taste of its waters.

The verse begins the south border. It runs from the wilderness of Zin along the Salt Sea. The southern line of the land is drawn.

So the witnesses see the bounding of the land begun on the south, from the wilderness of Zin by the Salt Sea. The witnesses note the starting point: “from the wilderness of Zin… the outmost coast of the salt sea eastward” — the south border, Gill observes, ran from east to west; it began at the southern end of the Salt Sea (the Dead Sea) and ran along by the edge of the wilderness of Zin and the border of Edom. They mark the identification of the sea, which the witnesses give: Clarke and Wesley name it the Dead Sea, the lake Asphaltites, called the “salt sea” from the salt and sulphurous taste of its waters; its eastern shore marked the corner whence the line set out. They observe Wesley’s note that the border is described from east to west by various windings and turnings, following the lie of the mountains and rivers, not a straight ruled line but the natural features of the land. They note that God begins the bounding at the south-east corner, by the Salt Sea, and carries the line westward toward the river of Egypt and the Great Sea (vv. 4–5). They observe the care the witnesses see in the exactness of the description: God marks every turn of the border, that his people might know precisely the land he gave, leaving nothing to dispute. They note that the very wilderness and the dead sea formed part of the appointed bound. They observe that the south, the side of Edom and the desert, was fixed first. The verse thus begins the south border — its turnings continued in the next verses.
4:
On Numbers 34:4 — “And your border shall turn from the south to the ascent of Akrabbim… to Kadeshbarnea… to Hazaraddar, and pass on to Azmon” — the witnesses mark the turnings of the south border.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the border turns to the ascent of Akrabbim, passes to Zin, and goes forth south of Kadesh-barnea to Hazar-addar and Azmon. Charles Ellicott: better, to the south side of the ascent of Akrabbim, the goings forth south of Kadesh-barnea. The Reformed Gill and Wesley: Azmon, at the west end of the mount of Edom.

The verse traces the turnings. The border bends by Akrabbim and Kadesh-barnea to Azmon. The southern line winds along Edom.

So the witnesses see the south border traced through its several turnings, by named passes and towns. The witnesses note the bend: “your border shall turn from the south to the ascent of Akrabbim” — Ellicott would render it “to the south side of the ascent of Akrabbim” (the “scorpion-pass”), a rugged ascent on the way up from the Arabah; the line turns there and passes on to Zin. They mark the further points: “the going forth thereof shall be… to Kadesh-barnea… to Hazar-addar… to Azmon” — Kadesh-barnea, the place of Israel’s long sojourn and the sending of the spies, lay near the border; the line ran south of it and on by Hazar-addar to Azmon, which Gill and Wesley place at the west end of the mountain of Edom. They observe the care of the witnesses to identify each name, though (Ellicott notes) the exact spots of some are no longer certain; the border followed a known and definite line of passes, towns, and wilderness. They note that the south border thus skirted the land of Edom and the southern desert, marking off Canaan from the wastes below it. They observe the providence the witnesses see in so exact a bounding: that God left his people in no doubt of the extent of their grant, the line drawn point by point. They note that the border, winding by the natural features, was both a real frontier and a divine appointment. They observe that Kadesh, the place of past unbelief, lay on the edge of the promised possession. The verse thus traces the turnings — the line reaching the river of Egypt and the sea in the next verse.
5:
On Numbers 34:5 — “And the border shall fetch a compass from Azmon unto the river of Egypt, and the goings out of it shall be at the sea” — the witnesses mark the south border ending at the river of Egypt and the sea.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the river of Egypt, the eastern branch of the Nile, or a river south of the Philistines. Charles Ellicott: the boundary ran along the brook of Egypt to the Mediterranean. The Reformed Gill: the border turns about, not in a straight line. Wesley: the river of Egypt, called Sihor (Joshua 13:3), dividing Egypt from Canaan; the sea, the midland sea.

The verse closes the south border. It curves by the river of Egypt to the Great Sea. The southern line reaches the sea.

So the witnesses see the south border complete its course, curving by the river of Egypt to the Mediterranean. The witnesses note the bend: “the border shall fetch a compass from Azmon unto the river of Egypt” — Gill observes that the line did not run straight but turned about, sweeping round from Azmon to the river or brook of Egypt. They mark the identification of that river, on which the witnesses differ a little: Clarke takes it for the eastern branch of the Nile, or a river south of the land of the Philistines; Wesley and others (after Joshua 13:3) name it Sihor, the brook of Egypt that divided Egypt from Canaan, the wady commonly held to be el-Arish, not the Nile itself. They observe the end of the line: “the goings out of it shall be at the sea” — the south border ran westward to the Great Sea (the Mediterranean), which Wesley calls the midland sea, “the sea” emphatically, the other “seas” of the land being but lakes. They note that the south-western corner of Canaan was thus fixed where the brook of Egypt met the sea, marking the land off from Egypt and the desert. They observe the breadth of the grant the witnesses see in this: that the promised land reached from the brook of Egypt, near the old house of bondage, to the great sea, a goodly and well-bounded inheritance. They note that the south border, traced from the Salt Sea to the Mediterranean, was now complete. The verse thus closes the south border at the river of Egypt and the sea — the west border following in the next verse.
6:
On Numbers 34:6 — “And as for the western border, ye shall even have the great sea for a border: this shall be your west border” — the witnesses mark the west border, the Great Sea.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the Mediterranean, called the Great Sea to distinguish it from the Dead Sea and the Sea of Tiberias, which were but lakes. Charles Ellicott: the great sea and its coast (Joshua 15:47). The Reformed Gill and Wesley: the midland sea, from south to north, running parallel with mount Lebanon.

The verse sets the west border. The Great Sea is the western bound. The Mediterranean coast bounds the land.

So the witnesses see the west border set simply at the Great Sea, the Mediterranean. The witnesses note the plainness of this bound: “ye shall even have the great sea for a border” — unlike the winding south and north lines, the western boundary needed no tracing of points, for the sea itself was the border, the whole western coast of Canaan washed by the Mediterranean. They mark the name and the reason for it: Clarke and Wesley call it the Great Sea, or the midland sea, so named to distinguish it from the lesser waters of the land — the Dead Sea, the Sea of Tiberias — which, though called “seas,” were but lakes; the Mediterranean alone was the great sea. They observe Ellicott’s note (after Joshua 15:47) that the border was “the great sea and its coast,” the shore-line and the bordering waters. They note the extent of the bound: the sea ran along the whole western side, from the brook of Egypt in the south to the region of Lebanon in the north, parallel (Wesley says) with mount Lebanon. They observe that God gave his people a sea-coast for their western frontier, a clear and unmistakable bound, and an opening (had they used it) toward the nations. They note the security the witnesses see in so plain a border: the great sea, fixed and unchanging, marked the western edge of the inheritance beyond all dispute. They observe that the west needed no more than to be named. The verse thus sets the west border at the Great Sea — the north border traced in the next verses.
7:
On Numbers 34:7 — “And this shall be your north border: from the great sea ye shall point out for you mount Hor” — the witnesses mark the north border and its Mount Hor.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the northern boundary cannot be determined with certainty; from the great sea mark out mount Hor. Charles Ellicott: not Hermon (too far east); some other height. The Reformed Gill and Wesley: not the Hor where Aaron died (that was southward), but another, thought to be a part of Lebanon, the northern border of the land.

The verse begins the north border. From the Great Sea they mark out Mount Hor. The northern line starts at a Lebanon height.

So the witnesses see the north border begun at a Mount Hor, distinct from the southern mountain of that name. The witnesses note the starting point: “from the great sea ye shall point out for you mount Hor” — the line of the north border was to be drawn from the Mediterranean coast inland to a mountain called Hor. They mark the careful distinction Wesley and Gill draw: this Mount Hor is not the Hor where Aaron died (Numbers 20:28), which lay far to the south by the border of Edom, but another mountain in the north — thought, they say, to be a part of the range of Lebanon, which is elsewhere named as the northern border of the land. They observe the uncertainty the witnesses honestly own: Keil and Delitzsch say the northern boundary cannot be determined with certainty, the ancient names being now obscure; Ellicott rejects the guess that it was Hermon (which stands too far eastward to suit the line) and supposes some other height of the Lebanon range. They note the general course the witnesses agree on: that the north border ran from the sea-coast eastward along the Lebanon, marking off Canaan from Syria and the lands beyond. They observe that, though the exact peak be unknown, the line was plain enough to Israel and is fixed by God’s appointment. They note that the northern frontier reached up to the great mountains, a goodly bound to the land. They observe that God set the limit there, that his people might know how far the grant extended. The verse thus begins the north border at Mount Hor — its further course traced in the next verses.
8:
On Numbers 34:8 — “From mount Hor ye shall point out your border unto the entrance of Hamath; and the goings forth of the border shall be to Zedad” — the witnesses mark the north border by the entrance of Hamath.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: from mount Hor to the entrance of Hamath, the goings forth to Zedad. Charles Ellicott: the line passed toward Hazar-enan (Ezekiel 47:16), probably crossing the northern Lebanon. The Reformed Gill: the entrance of Hamath, a narrow pass leading from the land of Canaan, by Hamath the great.

The verse continues the north border. From Mount Hor it runs to the entrance of Hamath and Zedad. The northern line passes the famous gateway.

So the witnesses see the north border carried on from Mount Hor to the entrance of Hamath. The witnesses note the course: “from mount Hor ye shall point out your border unto the entrance of Hamath” — the line ran from the mountain to the “entering in of Hamath,” a noted point often named as the northern limit of the land (Joshua 13:5). They mark Gill’s identification: the “entrance of Hamath” was a narrow pass leading out of the land of Canaan northward, by the great city of Hamath on the Orontes; the gateway between the Lebanon ranges through which the road to the north ran. They observe the further point: “the goings forth of the border shall be to Zedad” — the line passed on to Zedad and (the next verse) Ziphron and Hazar-enan; Ellicott connects this with the like description in Ezekiel 47:16, the border probably crossing the northern part of the Lebanon. They note the honest admission of the witnesses that several of these places are now unknown or uncertain, the names having perished; yet the general line, by the entrance of Hamath, is well marked. They observe that the north border thus reached the famous pass that was Israel’s gate toward the great northern powers; the land extended to the very threshold of the nations. They note that God set the bound at this natural and well-known gateway. They observe that the inheritance reached its full northern extent here. The verse thus continues the north border to the entrance of Hamath — the line going on in the next verse.
9:
On Numbers 34:9 — “And the border shall go on to Ziphron, and the goings out of it shall be at Hazar-enan: this shall be your north border” — the witnesses mark the end of the north border at Hazar-enan.

The Reformed Gill: the border goes on to Ziphron, called Zapherin in the Jerusalem Targum; Jerome says a city Zephyrium in Cilicia, but that seems too distant.

The verse closes the north border. The line ends at Hazar-enan. The northern bound is completed.

So the witnesses see the north border reach its end at Hazar-enan, the north-eastern corner of the land. The witnesses note the final points: “the border shall go on to Ziphron, and the goings out of it shall be at Hazar-enan” — the line passed from Zedad to Ziphron and ended at Hazar-enan, the “village of springs,” which marked the north-eastern corner whence the east border would descend. They mark Gill’s note on the uncertainty of Ziphron: the Jerusalem Targum calls it Zapherin, and Jerome thought it a city Zephyrium in Cilicia, though Gill judges that to lie too far off to suit the border of Canaan; the exact place is no longer sure. They observe that, the names being ancient and many now lost, the witnesses cannot fix every point, yet the line is clear in its course — from the great sea, by Mount Hor and the entrance of Hamath, to Hazar-enan. They note that Hazar-enan was the turning-point: here the north border ended and the east border began (v. 10), the corner of the land toward Damascus and the wilderness. They observe the completeness of the bounding the witnesses see: God carried the line all round, north as well as south, that the whole compass of the inheritance might be set down. They note that the northern frontier, reaching to the springs of Hazar-enan, marked the full extent of the land on that side. They observe that God left no side of the grant unbounded. The verse thus closes the north border at Hazar-enan — the east border beginning in the next verse.
10:
On Numbers 34:10 — “And ye shall point out your east border from Hazar-enan to Shepham” — the witnesses mark the beginning of the east border.

The Reformed Gill: from the place where the northern border ended (which Jerome says the Hebrews call Apamia); Shepham was a city between Hazar-enan and Riblah.

The verse begins the east border. From Hazar-enan the line runs to Shepham. The eastern bound is started.

So the witnesses see the east border begun at Hazar-enan, the corner where the north border had ended. The witnesses note the starting point: “ye shall point out your east border from Hazar-enan to Shepham” — the eastern line set out from the same Hazar-enan that closed the north border, running thence southward to Shepham; Gill observes (after Jerome) that the Hebrews called the place of the corner Apamia, and that Shepham was a city lying between Hazar-enan and Riblah, the next point on the line. They mark that the east border, like the others, is traced by named places, point to point, though several (Gill notes) are now hard to fix with certainty. They observe the turn the land’s bounding now takes: having gone up the south, west, and north, the line now comes down the east, to enclose the whole; the east border ran southward from the northern corner, by Shepham and Riblah, down to the Sea of Galilee and the Jordan and the Salt Sea (vv. 11–12). They note that this eastern line marked off Canaan proper, west of the Jordan, from the lands beyond — the inheritance of the two and a half tribes that lay on the east side. They observe the completeness the witnesses see: God drew the border all the way round, beginning and ending the circuit, that the land might be wholly enclosed and known. They note that the east, the side toward the wilderness and the river, was now bounded. They observe that the grant was being sealed on every side. The verse thus begins the east border at Hazar-enan — its course continued in the next verses.

So the witnesses see, in the beginning of the east border, the bounding of the land brought round its fourth and final side. They observe that the eastern line set out from Hazar-enan, the same northern corner where the north border had ended, and ran southward by Shepham and Riblah down toward the Sea of Galilee and the Jordan; the circuit of the land was thus carried, side by side, from the south to the west, to the north, and now down the east, until it should close again at the Salt Sea where it had begun. They note that this eastern line marked off Canaan proper, west of the Jordan, from the lands beyond the river, where the two and a half tribes had their portion; the Jordan and its valley formed the natural and appointed wall on that side. They mark that, the ancient names being many of them now lost or doubtful, the witnesses cannot fix every point with certainty, yet the line is clear in its course and sure in its divine appointment. They observe the completeness God intended: that the inheritance should be enclosed on every side, no border left open or undefined, the whole land measured and granted, that his people might possess a sure and bounded portion from his hand.
11:
On Numbers 34:11 — “And the coast shall go down from Shepham to Riblah, on the east side of Ain… unto the side of the sea of Chinnereth eastward” — the witnesses mark the east border by the Sea of Galilee.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the sea of Chinnereth, the same as the sea of Galilee, of Tiberias, of Gennesaret. Charles Ellicott: Riblah on the east of Ain, distinct from the Riblah of 2 Kings 25:6. The Reformed Gill: Riblah, said to be Daphne, near Antioch in the land of Hamath.

The verse traces the east border. The line descends by Riblah to the Sea of Chinnereth. The eastern bound reaches the Sea of Galilee.

So the witnesses see the east border descend from Shepham by Riblah to the Sea of Chinnereth. The witnesses note the course: “the coast shall go down from Shepham to Riblah, on the east side of Ain” — the line ran down from Shepham to Riblah, set on the east side of Ain (a fountain or spring); Ellicott would distinguish this Riblah from the famous Riblah in the land of Hamath where the kings of Babylon judged (2 Kings 25:6), and supposes Ain to be the great spring at the foot of Antilibanus. They mark Gill’s alternative, that Riblah was held by some to be Daphne, near Antioch in the land of Hamath. They observe the well-known goal of this stretch of the line: “the side of the sea of Chinnereth eastward” — Clarke identifies the Sea of Chinnereth as the same lake later called the Sea of Galilee, of Tiberias, and of Gennesaret; the border ran down to its eastern shore. They note the descent the witnesses trace: from the high northern country the line came down the east, by Riblah and Ain, to the freshwater sea of Galilee, and thence (the next verse) along the Jordan. They observe the providence the witnesses see in so exact a bounding, by spring and sea and river; God marked the eastern frontier by the very waters his people would later know so well. They note that the inland sea of Galilee lay on the eastern edge of the inheritance. They observe that the border followed the line of the Jordan valley downward. The verse thus traces the east border to the Sea of Chinnereth — the line reaching the Jordan and the Salt Sea in the next verse.
12:
On Numbers 34:12 — “And the border shall go down to Jordan, and the goings out of it shall be at the salt sea: this shall be your land with the coasts thereof round about” — the witnesses mark the east border ending at the Jordan and the Salt Sea, and the circuit complete.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the Jordan, famous in Old and New Testaments, rising at the foot of Lebanon, passing through Chinnereth, and emptying into the lake Asphaltites. The Reformed Gill: Jordan, a well-known river to the east of Canaan.

The verse closes the east border. The line goes down the Jordan to the Salt Sea, ending the circuit. The whole land is bounded round about.

So the witnesses see the east border, and with it the whole circuit of the land, completed at the Jordan and the Salt Sea. The witnesses note the final course: “the border shall go down to Jordan, and the goings out of it shall be at the salt sea” — from the Sea of Galilee the line ran down the river Jordan to the Salt Sea (the Dead Sea), where the south border had begun; the circuit was thus closed at the same south-eastern corner. They mark Clarke’s note on the famous river: the Jordan, renowned in both Testaments, takes its rise at the foot of Lebanon, passes through the Sea of Chinnereth, and empties into the lake Asphaltites; the river itself, for this stretch, was the eastern bound of Canaan proper. They observe the closing words that seal the whole description: “this shall be your land with the coasts thereof round about” — the four borders, south, west, north, and east, are now drawn, and the land is bounded on every side; the grant is complete and entire. They note the security the witnesses see in this: that God had compassed the inheritance about, marking it off wholly, that his people might possess a land whose every side was fixed by his word. They observe that the Jordan, the river of so many wonders, formed the eastern wall of the promised land. They note that the bounding, begun and ended at the Salt Sea, made one complete circuit. They observe that God gave a land defined and whole. The verse thus closes the east border and the circuit — the command to divide it following in the next verse.
13:
On Numbers 34:13 — “And Moses commanded the children of Israel, saying, This is the land which ye shall inherit by lot… which the LORD commanded to give unto the nine tribes, and to the half tribe” — the witnesses mark the land divided by lot among the nine tribes and a half.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: this land, emphatically described, full of advantages of climate, soil, and situation, to be inherited by lot. The Reformed Calvin: though a repetition, not superfluous; he contrasts the new allotment of the nine and a half tribes with the former grant of Bashan and Gilead east of Jordan.

The verse commands the division. The bounded land is to be inherited by lot by the nine tribes and a half. The grant is to be parted among them.

So the witnesses see Moses command that the bounded land be divided by lot among the nine tribes and the half tribe. The witnesses note the charge: “this is the land which ye shall inherit by lot” — the land just bounded on every side was to be apportioned, not by the people’s choice or strength, but by lot, that each tribe might receive its share as from the hand of God. They mark Clarke’s observation on the worth of the land thus granted: it is emphatically described as a land of many advantages — of climate, soil, and situation — a goodly inheritance, well worth the bounding and the dividing. They observe Calvin’s note that this command, though a repetition of what had been said, is not superfluous: it contrasts the new allotment of the nine tribes and a half, now to be made west of Jordan, with the former grant already given to the two and a half tribes (Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh) of Bashan and Gilead on the east side; the one division is set over against the other. They note the wisdom and fairness the witnesses see in the use of the lot: that the dividing by lot, under God’s overruling, prevented all envy and contention among the tribes, each receiving what God assigned, none able to complain (Proverbs 16:33). They observe the typology the witnesses suggest: that as each tribe had its lot in Canaan by God’s appointment, so the saints have their inheritance assigned them by God (Colossians 1:12). They note that the land was to be possessed as a gift parted by lot. The verse thus commands the division by lot — the eastern tribes’ portion noted in the next verses.
14:
On Numbers 34:14 — “For the tribe of the children of Reuben… and the tribe of the children of Gad… and half the tribe of Manasseh, have received their inheritance” — the witnesses mark the two and a half tribes already provided.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh have received their inheritance. The Reformed Gill: that tribe, and all the families belonging to it.

The verse names the eastern tribes. Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh have already received their inheritance. Two and a half tribes are provided for east of Jordan.

So the witnesses see the reason given why only nine tribes and a half were to share the land of Canaan: the other two and a half had already received their portion. The witnesses note the named tribes: “the tribe of the children of Reuben… the tribe of the children of Gad… and half the tribe of Manasseh” — these, with their families, had received their inheritance already, on the east side of Jordan. They mark the occasion the witnesses recall: when Israel came to the rich pasture-lands of Gilead and Bashan, conquered from Sihon and Og, the tribes of Reuben and Gad, having much cattle, had asked for that territory, and half Manasseh with them; Moses had granted it on condition they go over armed to help their brethren win Canaan (Numbers 32:33). They observe that, having thus their portion settled east of the Jordan, these tribes were not to share in the lot of the land west of it; the dividing by lot was for the nine and a half who remained unprovided. They note the order and fairness the witnesses see: God so arranged that every tribe should have its inheritance, the two and a half on the east, the nine and a half on the west, none left without a portion. They observe that the naming of the eastern tribes here both explains the number and confirms their settled grant. They note that the whole nation was thus to be provided for. The verse thus names the two and a half tribes already provided — their eastward portion confirmed in the next verse.
15:
On Numbers 34:15 — “The two tribes and the half tribe have received their inheritance on this side Jordan near Jericho eastward, toward the sunrising” — the witnesses mark the eastern portion confirmed.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: better, along the side or bank of Jordan (Numbers 32:19). The Reformed Gill: the tribes of Reuben and Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh.

The verse confirms the eastern grant. The two and a half tribes have their portion east of Jordan, toward the sunrise. Their inheritance lies across the river.

So the witnesses see the eastern portion of the two and a half tribes confirmed and located. The witnesses note the place: “on this side Jordan near Jericho eastward, toward the sunrising” — their inheritance lay across the Jordan from Canaan proper, on its eastern bank, over against Jericho, toward the rising of the sun; Ellicott would render “along the side or bank of Jordan,” the land stretching along the river’s eastern side (Numbers 32:19). They mark Gill’s plain naming of the tribes once more — Reuben, Gad, and the half tribe of Manasseh — whose portion was thus distinct from, and outside, the bounded land of Canaan just described. They observe the point the witnesses draw out: that the land west of Jordan, marked by the four borders, was the proper inheritance to be divided by lot, while the eastern tribes’ portion lay beyond that border, a possession granted earlier and on other terms. They note that this notice, set here, makes clear why the lot of Canaan was for nine tribes and a half only, the rest being already settled. They observe the order and completeness the witnesses see: God provided for the whole people, some east and some west of the river, each tribe in its appointed place. They note that the two and a half had their lot toward the sunrising, the nine and a half theirs in the land of promise proper. They observe that none of Israel was without an inheritance. The verse thus confirms the eastern portion — the men to divide the western land named in the next verses.
16:
On Numbers 34:16 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark God appointing the men to divide the land.

The grammatical-historical The Puritan Henry: God appoints commissioners for dividing the land; the conquest is taken for granted, though not a stroke yet struck; no generals are named, but dividers. The Reformed Gill: that the division might be done impartially and to satisfaction. Calvin: though the land was divided by lot, yet the authority of judges was required to order and confirm it.

The verse opens the appointment. God speaks to name the men who shall divide the land. The dividers are appointed before the conquest.

So the witnesses see God himself appoint the men who should divide the land among the tribes. The witnesses note the striking observation Henry makes: that God here names commissioners for the dividing of the land, while the conquest of it is simply taken for granted, though as yet not a single stroke had been struck toward winning it; and that God names not the generals and captains who should fight for the land, but the men who should parcel it out — so sure was the victory in God’s purpose that he ordered the division before the battle. They mark Gill’s reason for the appointment: that the division might be made in the most impartial manner, and to the satisfaction of all the tribes, by men of God’s own choosing. They observe Calvin’s answer to a question that arises: if the land was to be divided by lot, why were judges or dividers needed at all, as if there were anything for them to decide? — the answer being that the lot determined the place of each tribe, but the appointed men were needed to order the whole business, to measure the portions to the size of each tribe, and to give the lot its due authority and execution. They note the faith the witnesses see in the whole arrangement: God speaks of dividing a land not yet entered, as a thing certain; his people may reckon the promise as good as the possession. They observe that God provides for the orderly settling of the inheritance beforehand. They note that the appointment rested on God’s word. The verse thus opens the appointment of the dividers — their names given in the next verses.
17:
On Numbers 34:17 — “These are the names of the men which shall divide the land unto you: Eleazar the priest, and Joshua the son of Nun” — the witnesses mark Eleazar and Joshua set over the division.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Eleazar the priest and Joshua the son of Nun, with one prince of every tribe. Charles Ellicott: better, “which shall give or allot the land as an inheritance.” The Reformed Gill: they were to take possession in the people’s name and stead, as their representatives, and distribute it. Wesley: Eleazar to act in God’s name, to cast lots, to consult God in cases of difficulty, to do the whole business in a solemn and religious manner.

The verse names the chief dividers. Eleazar the priest and Joshua are set over the division. The high priest and the leader head the work.

So the witnesses see the two chief men named who should head the dividing of the land: Eleazar the priest and Joshua. The witnesses note the office Gill and Ellicott describe: these men were to “divide” or, better, “allot” the land as an inheritance to the people; they were to take possession in the people’s name and stead, as their representatives, and then distribute it among the tribes. They mark the fitness of the two chosen: Eleazar the high priest and Joshua the son of Nun, the spiritual head and the civil-military head of the nation, joined together over the work; the dividing was to be both a sacred and a public act, ordered by the highest authorities. They observe Wesley’s account of Eleazar’s special part: the priest was to act in God’s name, to cast the lots, to prevent contention, to consult God by the Urim in any case of difficulty, and to transact the whole business in a solemn and religious manner; the lot was to be drawn under God’s eye and by his ordinance. They note the honour put on Joshua, who, having led the people into the land, should also have a chief hand in dividing it among them. They observe the order the witnesses see: that so weighty a matter as the parting of the inheritance was committed to men of God’s naming, the priest and the leader, that it might be done in the fear of God and to the content of all. They note that God set the work in worthy hands. The verse thus names the chief dividers — the princes added in the next verses.
18:
On Numbers 34:18 — “And ye shall take one prince of every tribe, to divide the land by inheritance” — the witnesses mark a prince of each tribe joined to the work.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: besides Eleazar and Joshua, one chief man or prince out of each of the ten tribes interested in the division. The Reformed Gill: one prince out of every tribe, that is, of the nine tribes and a half; none of Reuben or Gad, having had their inheritance elsewhere.

The verse adds the princes. One prince of each tribe is joined to divide the land. Each tribe has a hand in the work.

So the witnesses see one prince of every tribe added to Eleazar and Joshua, to share in the dividing of the land. The witnesses note the addition: “ye shall take one prince of every tribe” — besides the high priest and the leader, a chief man or prince was to be chosen from each of the tribes that were to receive a portion; Ellicott counts them as one from each of the ten tribes interested in the division (the nine and a half west of Jordan). They mark Gill’s clarification of which tribes: the princes were taken from the nine tribes and a half who were to inherit Canaan, but none from Reuben or Gad, who had already received their inheritance east of Jordan and so had no part in this division. They observe the wisdom and fairness the witnesses see in this arrangement: that each tribe should have its own prince among the dividers, so that no tribe might suspect itself wronged or another favoured; the presence of a representative from every interested tribe secured the impartiality and the acceptance of the division. They note the joining of authorities: the priest (the sacred), the leader (the public), and the princes (the tribal) together, that the parting of the inheritance might be done with the consent and oversight of all. They observe that God provided not only for the fairness of the lot but for the satisfaction of the people, every tribe owning a hand in the work. They note that the division was thus made a common and trusted act. The verse thus adds the princes to the work — their names beginning in the next verse.
19:
On Numbers 34:19 — “And the names of the men are these: Of the tribe of Judah, Caleb the son of Jephunneh” — the witnesses mark the naming of the princes, beginning with Caleb of Judah.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: Moses does not follow the order of the tribes used in chapters 1, 7, or 26, but places them otherwise. The Reformed Gill: the men were not left to the tribes to choose, but were named by the LORD himself, who best knew their fitness. Wesley: the order here is conformed to the order of their inheritances, which afterward fell by lot — a demonstration of the infinite foreknowledge of God.

The verse names the first prince. Caleb of Judah heads the list of dividers. The naming begins with the faithful spy.

So the witnesses see the princes named, beginning with Caleb of the tribe of Judah. The witnesses note the divine choosing: “the names of the men are these” — Gill observes that these men were not left to the tribes to elect, but were named by the LORD himself, who best knew their capacities and qualifications for so weighty a service; the dividers were of God’s appointment, man by man. They mark the fitness of the first named: “of the tribe of Judah, Caleb the son of Jephunneh” — Caleb, the faithful spy who with Joshua had brought back a good report and wholly followed the LORD (Numbers 14:24), is set at the head of the princes, of the tribe that led the camp; the man who had believed God concerning the land is honoured to help divide it. They observe the striking point Clarke and Wesley draw from the order of the names: Moses does not follow the order of the tribes used in the earlier lists (ch. 1, 7, 26), but ranges them otherwise — and Wesley notes that the order here is conformed to the order of their several inheritances, as these afterward fell to them by lot in the land; so that the list, written before the lot was cast, agrees with the lot that God would give, an evident demonstration of the infinite foreknowledge of God. They note the providence the witnesses see: that God, who would assign the lots, already knew and ordered their places, the dividers named in the very order their tribes would inherit. They observe that Caleb’s faith was crowned with this honour. The verse thus names the first prince, Caleb of Judah — the rest of the princes following in the verses after.
29:
On Numbers 34:29 — “These are they whom the LORD commanded to divide the inheritance unto the children of Israel in the land of Canaan” — the witnesses mark the dividers commissioned by the LORD.

The Reformed Gill: the LORD not only named and appointed them, but laid his commands upon them, and obliged them; even this order was made before the land was conquered.

The verse closes the list. These are they whom the LORD commanded to divide the inheritance. The dividers stand commissioned by God.

So the witnesses see the list of the dividers sealed with the note that they were commissioned by the LORD himself. The witnesses note the divine authority Gill draws out: “these are they whom the LORD commanded” — God not only named and appointed these men (Eleazar, Joshua, and the twelve princes), but laid his express command upon them, binding and obliging them to the work; the dividing of the land was theirs by divine charge, not human arrangement. They mark the thing the witnesses find remarkable in the timing: that this whole order — the bounds of the land, the men to divide it, their very names and ranks — was made and recorded before the land was conquered, before Israel had so much as crossed the Jordan or struck a blow; God provided for the parting of the inheritance while it was yet wholly in enemy hands, so sure was the promise. They observe the purpose declared: “to divide the inheritance unto the children of Israel in the land of Canaan” — the appointed men were to apportion the bounded land among the tribes, each its share, as the LORD had commanded. They note the assurance the witnesses see in this for God’s people: that the inheritance was as good as theirs already, the dividers named, the work charged, the promise certain; faith might count the land won before the war began. They observe that God orders the future of his people with a sure hand. They note that the chapter ends, as it began, in the LORD’s command. The verse thus closes the list: the dividers commissioned by the LORD — the bounds and the parting of the inheritance now fully ordered.
